Case Study 01 / 02

Joyous Chat — personalized journaling, without the chat

FIGMA · ADOBE SUITE
PRODUCT DESIGN INTERN

The problem

Journaling is one of the most consistently recommended tools for emotional processing, yet most people who start a journaling habit abandon it within weeks — the blank page is intimidating, and generic prompts don't adapt to a person's personality type and circumstances. Joyous wanted to test whether an AI-guided chat interface could keep people journaling longer by making reflection feel more personalised.

What it does

Joyous Chat is an AI-based, non-conversational journaling app. It analyzes a user's personality type and needs across specific target areas — relationships, career, motivation, family, friendships, daily routines — and synthesizes that with psychological frameworks (behavior-change theory, social and personal motivation theory, and established journaling methodologies) to generate a personalised, targeted journaling experience.

Why non-conversational

We deliberately designed away from a back-and-forth chat. Conversational framing risks humanizing the AI and building an unreliable emotional dependency on software, and it opens the door to the AI giving unethical or incorrect medical or therapeutic advice. Keeping the format one-directional — the app asks, the person reflects in their own words — let us keep the depth of a guided conversation without those risks.

Who it's for

Students and early-career adults who wanted a lower-friction way to build emotional-processing habits, but who had tried and dropped traditional journaling apps before — people who needed the app to meet them where they were, not the other way around.

Role
Position
Product Design Intern & Researcher
Responsibilities
End-to-end UX: research → wireframes → prototype → design system
Research
60+ user interviews, usability testing
Iterations
5+ full design cycles
Outcome
User-reported helpfulness 70% → 85%
Presented to
Faculty, partners & peers — academic symposium
Process

Design moved through five cycles, each grounded in a new round of interviews and usability testing rather than assumption. Early conversations surfaced the real barrier — not motivation, but the cold-start problem of "what do I even say?" — which reframed the whole direction of the product.

01
Ethnographic research
60+ interviews on journaling habits, drop-off points, and emotional triggers.
02
Synthesis
Coded findings into design requirements shared with engineering & research.
03
Low-fi wireframes
Mapped the personality-assessment and target-area selection flow.
04
Usability testing
5+ cycles refining flow, accessibility, and clarity of prompts.
05
Clinical prototype
Responsive Figma prototype synthesizing personality data with behavior-change and motivation theory into targeted prompts.
Design iteration — from static prompt to theory-driven questions

The interaction model went through three meaningful shifts: a static prompt list, then a guided Q&A where the person picks a focus area, and finally deep, theory-informed questions that address the specific difficulty within that chosen area by drawing on psychological frameworks — each step tested for comprehension and accessibility before moving forward.

V1 · Static prompt
"What made you smile today?"
"Describe a challenge."
"One word for today."
Low completion — felt like homework
V3 · Guided Q&A
What would you like to focus on today?
Relationships · Career · Motivation
Family · Friendships · Routines
Better retention, still generic per topic
V5 · Theory-driven question
You're working on Relationships. Attachment research suggests early patterns shape how we connect now — where do you notice that pattern showing up today?
[ journal entry — free response ]
Shipped direction — helpfulness ↑

Wireframe reconstructions illustrating the interaction model's evolution across design cycles.

Shipped screens

The screens below are from the live product: the entry point, the personality assessment that sets each person's target areas, and a sample journal entry showing the final, theory-informed prompt in place.

*Changes in progress — screens reflect the current build and will keep evolving as the product ships.

"Presenting the clinical prototype at the symposium was the first time I had to defend a design decision to people outside design — that's what made the rationale as important as the pixels."

Case Study 02 / 02

PScience.intnl — psychology, made scroll-stoppable

ADOBE ILLUSTRATOR
TIKTOK · INSTAGRAM
2022–2023

The problem

Psychology content online tends to sit at two extremes: dense academic writing that nobody outside the field reads, or oversimplified "life hack" posts with no rigor behind them. I wanted to design a visual system that could carry real psychological concepts — defense mechanisms, attachment styles, communication research — in a format built for a nine-second attention span.

Who it's for

Gen Z and young-adult social media users who are curious about psychology but won't read a textbook — an audience that decides whether to keep reading within the first screen, so hierarchy and pacing had to do the work a headline usually does.

Role
Position
Founder & Creative/Design Lead
Team
Directed designers, writers & researchers
Reach
30,000+ users across TikTok & Instagram
Deliverables
Brand system, carousel templates, illustration set
Recognition
CAYLA Social Innovation Exhibition (11% acceptance)
Visual system

Every post follows the same structural logic: a high-contrast opening card states the concept, mid-card panels alternate background/foreground color to pace the read like a comic strip, and a closing card lands on an emotional beat rather than a summary. Color was assigned by content mood — maroon and teal for weightier topics like trauma and defense mechanisms, lavender and peach for lighter, everyday-relationship content — so a reader could gauge tone before reading a word.

Selected posts
"Directing a team meant the system had to survive contact with other people's hands — templates, not one-off files, so anyone on the team could ship on-brand in an afternoon."
30K+
Users reached
11%
CAYLA acceptance rate

Team disciplines directed
